sections.summary: Document title: Standard for Luncheon Meat (CXS 89-1981).

sections.section: Adopted in 1981. Revised in 1991, 2014, 2015. Amended in 2019, 2022.
sections.summary: Records adoption and revision history: adopted 1981; revised 1991, 2014, 2015; amended 2019 and 2022.

Amendment adopted at the 45th Codex Alimentarius Commission (December 2022) replacing the previous Section 7.4 text on labelling of non‑retail containers with a requirement that such labelling be in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).
Scope: applies to products designated "Luncheon Meat" that have been packed in any suitable packing material.

Describes Luncheon Meat as a comminuted, cured (and optionally smoked) product prepared from meat and/or poultry that may contain binders, and requires that heat treatment, curing and packaging be sufficient to prevent public health hazards and keep the product wholesome during storage, transport and sale.

Defines subsidiary terms: 'edible offal' (includes lungs except if lungs are from scalded animals, includes poultry skin, and excludes specified parts such as ears, snouts, mucous membrane, sinews, genital system, udders, intestines and bladder), 'meat', 'packaged', and 'poultry meat' for the purposes of the standard.

Specifies essential and optional ingredients and composition/quality requirements: essential ingredients are meat/poultry (excluding edible offal), water and curing agents; optional ingredients include offal, fats, binders, sugars, spices and hydrolysed proteins; minimum ingoing meat content is 80% with binder and 90% without binder; raw materials must be fit for consumption and free of objectionable odours/flavours and the final product must be clean, substantially free from staining/contamination, uniformly cured and sliceable.
Lists essential ingredients required for Luncheon Meat: meat or poultry meat (excluding edible offal), water, and curing ingredients consisting of food‑grade salt and sodium or potassium nitrite.

Lists optional ingredients permitted in Luncheon Meat, including edible offal, fats, carbohydrate and protein binders (e.g., flours, milk powders, vegetable proteins), sugars and syrups, spices/seasonings/condiments, and water‑soluble aromatic hydrolysed protein.
Composition requirement table: minimum ingoing meat content is 80% for products with a binder and 90% for products without binder (which may include heart, tongue or head meat from mammals).

Requires raw materials to be suitable for human consumption and free from objectionable odours and flavours, and requires the final product to be clean, substantially free from staining or container contamination, uniformly and thoroughly cured, and capable of being sliced.
States that ingredients used as raw materials must be of a quality suitable for human consumption and free from objectionable odours and flavours.
Specifies final product quality: the product must be clean, substantially free from staining and contamination from the container, uniformly and thoroughly cured, and capable of being sliced.

Permits preservatives, humectants and colours when used in accordance with Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192‑1995) for food category 08.3.2 and allows only certain Table 3 additives as indicated; use of flavourings must follow the Guidelines for the Use of Flavourings (CXG 66‑2008) and the carry‑over conditions in Section 4.1 of CXS 192‑1995 apply.

Contaminants requirement: products must comply with the Maximum Levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193‑1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides and veterinary drugs.

Hygiene recommendation: products should be prepared and handled in accordance with relevant Codex texts (e.g., General Principles of Food Hygiene CXC 1‑1969, Code of Hygienic Practice for Meat CXC 58‑2005, canned foods and Listeria guidance) and should comply with any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21‑1997.

Labelling requirements: the name of the food must be declared as "Luncheon Meat" and declarations of binders, edible offal and the species of origin are required where omission would mislead; the ingredients list must indicate species, date‑marking rules differ for shelf‑stable versus non‑shelf‑stable products, and labelling of non‑retail containers should follow CXS 346‑2021.
Label must declare the name of the food as "Luncheon Meat", and a declaration of binders, edible offal and the animal species must be given with the name if omitting them would mislead the consumer.

Date of minimum durability must be shown by year for shelf-stable products; for non-shelf-stable products (expected to keep <18 months) in consumer or catering packages it must be shown by day, month and year; for non-shelf-stable products in containers not sold directly to consumers or catering, adequate storage and distribution instructions shall be declared.
Labelling of non-retail containers should be in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).

Compliance with this Standard shall be checked using the methods of analysis and sampling contained in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to the Standard's provisions.
